compounds.name: AHK-Cu
compounds.beginnerSummary: AHK-Cu is a copper-complexed Ala-His-Lys peptide identity. For PeptideOS, it belongs as a topical-first skin-hair tracking compound: verify the label, capture formulation details, track scalp or skin response, and keep the evidence framed as ex vivo/in vitro rather than clinical proof.
compounds.researcherDetails: AHK-Cu is a copper-complexed Ala-His-Lys peptide identity. For PeptideOS, it belongs as a topical-first skin-hair tracking compound: verify the label, capture formulation details, track scalp or skin response, and keep the evidence framed as ex vivo/in vitro rather than clinical proof.
compounds.mechanism: dermal papilla cell proliferation, human hair follicle ex vivo elongation, apoptosis-signaling markers in cultured dermal papilla cells, copper-peptide identity and labeling
compounds.safety: topical: Irritation potential depends on concentration, carrier base, excipients, and individual skin or scalp sensitivity. topical: Label confusion with GHK-Cu or generic copper peptide blends can corrupt inventory, schedule, and response tracking. topical: Raw cosmetic ingredients require formulation context before they can be treated like a finished topical product. topical: Long-term outcomes for consumer topical AHK-Cu products are formulation-specific and not resolved by the reviewed sources. topical: Product-specific stability, storage, and carrier compatibility require label or supplier documentation.
compounds.storage: Verify storage and handling from the labeled product or source-backed container details.

citations.title: The effect of tripeptide-copper complex on human hair growth in vitro

biohackerBrief.headline: Topical-first copper peptide with preclinical hair-follicle signal.
whyPeopleCare: It is commonly discussed by biohackers in the same copper-peptide lane as GHK-Cu, but the identity is different.
The strongest source-backed signal located here is hair-follicle and dermal-papilla cell research, which makes scalp tracking a natural app use case.
It gives Peppi a useful guardrail: do not assume injectable workflows for every peptide-like product.
verifyBeforeUse: Confirm the product says AHK-Cu or L-alanyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine-Cu2+, not GHK-Cu or Copper Tripeptide-1.
Confirm whether the item is a finished topical product or a raw cosmetic ingredient.
Capture concentration, carrier base, lot number, expiration date, and storage instructions from the label.
Verify any claimed human outcome data separately from the 2007 ex vivo/in vitro paper.
trackInApp: Scalp or skin photos at user-chosen checkpoints.
Local tolerability, irritation notes, and formulation or carrier changes.
Product batch, lot number, and expiration changes.
biohackerBrief.realityCheck: AHK-Cu has a real cataloged identity and a peer-reviewed ex vivo/in vitro hair-follicle paper, but this record does not support injectable use or clinical outcome claims for finished topical products.

referenceProfile.reviewSummary: AHK-Cu is a copper-complexed Ala-His-Lys peptide identity. For PeptideOS, it belongs as a topical-first skin-hair tracking compound: verify the label, capture formulation details, track scalp or skin response, and keep the evidence framed as ex vivo/in vitro rather than clinical proof.
mechanismTargets: human hair follicle ex vivo elongation
apoptosis-signaling markers in cultured dermal papilla cells

clinicalEvidence.finding: PubChem catalogs AHK-Cu as CID 168431292 with the formula C15H24ClCuN6O4- and molecular weight 451.39.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Database identity does not establish route, product quality, tolerability, or user outcomes.
CAS-style identity helps label verification but does not validate any protocol or finished product claim.

clinicalEvidence.finding: The 2007 AHK-Cu paper evaluated human hair follicles ex vivo and reported follicle elongation in that laboratory model.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Ex vivo follicle work is not a human clinical outcome trial and should not be displayed as proven topical product efficacy.

clinicalEvidence.finding: The 2007 AHK-Cu paper evaluated cultured dermal papilla cells and reported proliferation and apoptosis-marker findings.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Cell-culture findings are mechanistic context only; they do not establish clinical benefit, route, or formulation performance.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Topical AHK-Cu tracking should capture formulation details because user experience depends on the finished product, carrier, excipients, and concentration.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: This is an app-tracking inference from formulation variability, not a compound-specific clinical safety claim.

safetySignals: topical: Irritation potential depends on concentration, carrier base, excipients, and individual skin or scalp sensitivity.
topical: Label confusion with GHK-Cu or generic copper peptide blends can corrupt inventory, schedule, and response tracking.
topical: Raw cosmetic ingredients require formulation context before they can be treated like a finished topical product.
topical: Long-term outcomes for consumer topical AHK-Cu products are formulation-specific and not resolved by the reviewed sources.
topical: Product-specific stability, storage, and carrier compatibility require label or supplier documentation.
practicalNotes: Track AHK-Cu as its own copper-peptide identity instead of merging it with GHK-Cu.
Keep topical scalp or skin product data tied to the exact label, carrier, and site response.
Separate preclinical hair-follicle signal from unsupported protocol claims.
Users comparing topical copper-peptide products for scalp or skin appearance goals.
Users who want Peppi to catch AHK-Cu versus GHK-Cu label ambiguity before adding inventory.
Users documenting product-specific response notes, photos, and tolerability over time.
Verify whether a label is AHK-Cu, GHK-Cu, or a generic copper peptide blend.
Track scalp or skin site response without treating the compound as automatically injectable.
Compare product batches, carrier bases, and irritation notes.
application site
scalp or skin photos
local irritation or tolerability notes
shedding or hair-density notes when relevant
carrier base and formulation changes
product batch changes
Inventory field: exact label name
Inventory field: CAS number or PubChem CID when present
Inventory field: concentration
Inventory field: carrier base
Inventory field: finished product versus raw ingredient
Inventory field: lot number
Inventory field: expiration date
evidenceGaps: No reviewed source in this pass establishes subcutaneous, intramuscular, oral, or intranasal AHK-Cu use.

Amass TrialCore searches for AHK-Cu and L-alanyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine did not surface a matching AHK-Cu clinical trial record; returned records were unrelated copper, CU-code, or alanyl-glutamine studies.
Human clinical outcomes for finished topical AHK-Cu products are not established by the cited 2007 ex vivo/in vitro study.
Product-specific tolerability and performance depend on the finished formulation, not just the AHK-Cu identity.

regulatoryStatus.summary: No approved-label status is asserted by this source-backed record.
regulatoryStatus.limitations: Regulatory status must be verified from source-backed records before display as approved.

peptideOSActions: Read a product photo and ask the user to confirm whether the label says AHK-Cu, GHK-Cu, or a generic copper peptide.
Add a labeled AHK-Cu topical product to inventory after user confirmation.
Flag unsupported route assumptions if the user tries to log AHK-Cu as injectable without source-backed product details.
Create reminders from user-confirmed label instructions without inventing frequency or protocol guidance.
Summarize site notes, photos, and batch changes for user review.
